critiques.author: R3
critiques.content: imo it’s pretty good, although the wsib bullet points are very hand-wavey and doesn’t really highlight any real technical proficiencies

critiques.author: R3
critiques.content: i would go deeper into implementation side as to how you achieved parity with llama2 vs gpt4, because that in itself is actually pretty cool if you can communicate it well

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Cater the resume to the jobs you want to apply for

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: AI is featured prominently in your resume, cut that down

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Web stuff is featured prominently, cut that down

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Expand on the roles that have the relevant experience

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: At intern/co-op, we don’t really expect your leadership skills to be good, so you can mention it but don’t go overboard

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Also the resume just has many words. The details under job roles could be more concise

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Like, 'utilized the atlassian API along with' is so much fluff. Start your sentences/bullets with, what did you build, followed by the tech

critiques.author: mcpenguin
critiques.content: I personally think leadership (and other soft skills) is good because it shows you have initiative and that you've taken charge of a project before, which is something that a lot of devs lack. most of my good coop evaluations have been because i displayed a drive and often took charge of a project, and at large companies interns are often expected to take initiative of a project for the duration of their internship

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: we just want to see that you finished a project, so as long as that happened, we assume you lead a project

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: most bigger/reasonable tech internships we give a dedicated project for the intern to own from start to finish, so this is more of the norm

critiques.content: im more interested in roles similar to my projects (C/C++ low level systems) stuff but my exp doesnt rlly align with that sadly. so hard to get those roles

critiques.content: Write a like “objective statement” at the start that indicates your interest

critiques.author: mcpenguin
critiques.content: save that space for elaborating on your other stuff

critiques.author: mcpenguin
critiques.content: a good recruiter is gonna have a good idea of your "objective" based on your other stuff and the job youre applying to anyways

critiques.author: mcpenguin

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: sure but this resume is a hodgepodge so it will be confusing as to whether the candidate is aligned to the role or not

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: and if you're very clearly wanting one type of role instead of another, having a very succinct oneliner at the top will indicate to the recruiter whether you should be considered for non low level systems roles or not

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: (in this case, it sounds like you would NOT want to be considered for those roles)

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: I rarely suggest objective statements, but in this case because it's a varied mix, and the most recent experience is not low level systems, it's best to make that clear up front

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: also, lol good recruiters, don't assume there are good recruiters; assume you have recruiters are doing quick keyword scanning

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: reduce the lines of the first maybe second role, def elaborate the last 2 roles since they are more relevant

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Recommendations are just that, recommendations. In most cases they’re good but sometimes they’re not! They’re not rules. You know best yourself and your resume and your career goals. So do what you think makes the most sense to get you the job you want.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Explain the features, and explain what the apps do

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: No one “architects” tests; just say you wrote 100+ tests

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: I dunno if “ensures end to end functionality” is meaningful as a phrase; you can instead talk about whether you improved code coverage, or made the edge case testing more robust or something

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Like, lol, yes the reason we write tests is to gain confidence in end to end testing, news at 11

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: What does “spearheaded” mean

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: ETL processes and SQL scripts reads funny; do you want you explain more what that means?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: The first job is p reasonable, but just reads a lil clunky. You’re stuffing a lot of impactful but a teeny bit generic info there. I’d recommend you revisit and think about the narrative of your intern /co-op project there and use that to guide the writing of that role. What were you brought on to solve and why?

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: Thanks for the feedback!

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: I definitely should be more specific with what the tests I wrote specifically improved on the product

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: just like data transformation rules to migrate data to Power BI

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: i guess ppl might not be familiar with what ETL stands for?

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: this makes sense, I will try to restructure my points accordingly. ig it just feels like I'm saying too much fluff?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: No you have a lot of good content here, just the word structure feels not smooth; there’s a lot of types of things you worked on that you’re writing as either an afterthought or you’re bundling with not like items. Framing matters here, so think about what narrative you want to present and it will come naturally to you what you should say.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Every resume tells a narrative about the person. What their history is, what their background is, but also what interests them and drives them. I see a hint of these in yours, but it’s a little jumbled. 🙂

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: People are familiar with ETLs, but “SQL scripts” probably undersells what you actually did. You wrote SQL for this, not SQL scripts.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Utilized X scripts is very passive in action description, you’re not committing in your sentences. Better to say Wrote X directly.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Passive shows that you’re hesitating or worried about taking credit for something. Take the credit! You assume you were the main one doing the project. 🙂

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: Hi, just wondering if you could take a look at an updated version of my resume?

critiques.author: hoomas
critiques.content: Just wanna say thanks for previous feedback again! very eye-opening

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content: I would definitely quantify your co-op experience. You were in the position for a few terms, so you should've seen the impact of your projects. critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I agree that the experience needs to be fleshed out if it was 3 whole terms. Right now, your actual work is very vague and the focus is just on listing tech stacks. For example, your first bullet spends 2 whole lines to just tell me you used React Native. "improving app functionality and delivering a seamless, consistent user experience across device" isn't specific enough to tell me anything about what you actually did
critiques.original_text: improving app functionality and delivering a seamless, consistent user experience across device

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Without any specific features/improvements I can't tell what the scope of your work was. Most of the current version reads as filler to me

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: For the ww job rec system, what does it actually do? Recommend jobs for people to apply to? Based on what? Resume? What does top 10% match accuracy mean? The second bullet is filler again. What are "more accurate job matches"? Like the rank-match kind of match? You're describing what the tool should ideally do (enhance UX, optimize job search), but you haven't told me enough of what it does to believe you

critiques.author: rayhaan

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content: Thats super impressive for 1b!! I would maybe add some metrics for the CSC discord bot (i.e. how many ppl are in the server/how many ppl are using it). Also this is personal preference, but I would avoid having only 1 or 2 words on a line, either add more or make it more concise

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I think you're missing some commas between items in your frameworks line. I would reconsider the order of your projects. IMO it should be most impressive/valuable first. So probably journalytics first, then graphing calc, then discord bot, then portfolio. But you can run multiple versions for different jobs. Similarly, some of your bullet points can be shuffled for importance. For projects, I like to open with a description of the project then more technical details later. In Journalytics, you have the description second

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: You could also expand on "Wrote a recursive equation evaluation algorithm to compare function values with points on the graph". I assume this is to calculate how to colour different points on the graph? This project could be a good differentiator since generic full stack/discord bot projects are common, so give it more focus if you can

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: someone suggested that i move the first open-source project to experience. i'm kinda on the edge about that since while i think its somewhat substantial contribution over multiple years, i'm worried employers will see placing it in experience as somewhat suspicious. would appreciate additional opinions on whether this is reasonable as a 1b with no other 'real' experience

critiques.author: refrigerated_microwave
critiques.content:
you were one of the 10 people who got the national scholarship?? actually crazy

i really dont think experience vs projects matters. if you were a recruiter, would you care if someone called their contributions as a project or experience? i agree a single experience is weird though

the first word of your bullet points changes from past tense to present tense a lot (e.g. contribute in the first bullet, but migrated in the second). they should all be past

your action verbs can also be better. you can do better than "work"

skills should be at the top, it contains the most concise and important info. also, if you show your skills first, then people will know what to look for in your bullets, instead of the employer skimming your bullet points without knowing the summary of your skills, reading your skills, and having to go back and think "wait, did this guy have ts experience again?"

add racket to languages, it's required to graduate

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content:
> the first word of your bullet points changes from past tense to present tense a lot (e.g. contribute in the first bullet, but migrated in the second). they should all be past
i used present to describe stuff i'm still actively doing and past tense for previous completed work, but i also agree the switch is mildly jarring

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: appreciate the rest of the advice, all sounds very reasonable to me

critiques.author: refrigerated_microwave
critiques.content: even for the present, you can still do past tense. youre saying "i've done this so far, and im also continuing"

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: to give an example, i don't know if "Maintained ..." accurately reflects that i am still actively developing the project, responding to bug reports, etc

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: at least to me it seems to suggest a project that's totally done

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: i do appreciate the perspective though and like i said i definitely agree the tense change reads a little oddly, i'm just thinking about whether changing some of it to past tense would be inaccurate (and how much that would matter)

critiques.author: refrigerated_microwave
critiques.content: that's fair, to me its not clear that you are trying to show your continued work. i thought it was just a typo since 99% of other resumes have past tense only. you could definitely make your method work, but it is a small leap of faith

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content:
yep absolutely its a pretty subtle distinction now that you bring it up. i wonder if there are better ways to surface this info... i know the usual advice is current job => all present tense and past job => past tense, but i'm not really sure what to do for a mix between the two. will ask around a little more

edit: for now i updated everything to past tense, save the "maintain" line which i switched to "actively maintaining". i think this is reasonably consistent. thanks again for the advice, not something i would've noticed myself since the tenses made sense in my head

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Only nitpick I have here is "all advanced courses" feels like uw math faculty lingo that I don't think anybody will understand. I sympathize with wanting to flex 14X on your resume but I would cut that

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Nuts resume for 1b tho

critiques.author: joe
critiques.content: very reasonable point about it being a little too specific, i will probably remove it. thanks

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Imo this is too long. Try to stick to A4/letter size. It should be easy to get there if you cut the Java Note project and tighten up some other bullets so they don't spill to 2 lines. Couple spelling issues I saw. "Lightweight but delicated React" delicate? "and perferring CSS" preferring? The projects are interesting and I like the succinct bullets that still have numbers/details. If there's room to give more technical details about interesting things you did to implement certain features that would be nice. But adding that might mean you need to cut more projects/bullets to fit in A4.

context_message:
Hey,
I'm in 3A, looking for general SWE co-ops for S25. 
Any feedback would be appreciated :) thanks!

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- The margins are too tight IMO. This is so tight that it won't print on most printers
- Why do you have a safety standards wiki page linked? Anybody who doesn't already know what that is won't care to learn (or at least link the actual gov webpage). The blue highlight draws the eye to this mostly irrelevant part of your resume
- What do you mean you reduced redundancy by 30% by applying SOLID? Is that 30% LoC reduction? What does script accuracy refer to? Reducing outstanding tasks by 10% is also a suspect stat (whether it means anything will depend entirely on the way your team uses jira) but at least I understand what you mean
- I personally am against using adverbs and/or describing the skills you demonstrated in your resume. E.g. "Demonstrated leadership skills by..." or "Proficiently utilized...". Tell me what you did and I'll decide how to interpret it. Those phrases are empty. I might even disagree with you about what skills you demonstrated.
- 20-30 min playtime is also a weird stat because that's more a function of how much level content you made than how many technical features you built. But I could see leaving it in
- The number of epochs over which you trained a model is meaningless on its own. Epoch isn't a well defined term so this doesn't tell me anything about the compute/data efficiency of your architecture.
- IMO 84% is a bit low of a CAV to highlight on your resume. For reference, you need an 85% CAV to be a marker for 1st year math, so that's at least 1 bar you would be DQed for.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I think your P2P network project description is the best one here. It avoids the vague descriptive language to be concise about what you built and uses concrete terms to describe tech/patterns involved (as opposed to listing high level design patterns like OOP which are too vague imo). Although it doesn't have any stats, which is fair for a hackathon project, I think you should try to use this style more

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
There's also enough fat to cut that you can grow your margins without losing content. E.g. your image classifier project can be redone in 1 line:
Built [deep learning model/some other descriptor] using Pytorch and Keras trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset, achieving 85% accuracy
or
Achieved 85% classification accuracy on the CIFAR-10 dataset using an ML model built with Pytorch and Keras

critiques.author: asterbot
critiques.content:
Hey - thanks for taking the time to write such a detailed feedback! really appreciate it.

> Why do you have a safety standards wiki page linked? Anybody who doesn't already know what that is won't care to learn (or at least link the actual gov webpage). The blue highlight draws the eye to this mostly irrelevant part of your resume
I was initially gonna word it with something like "ensuring compliance with industry standards" but that felt too vague so i changed it to this lol. I see your point about it drawing unnecessary attention.

> What do you mean you reduced redundancy by 30% by applying SOLID? Is that 30% LoC reduction?
Yea it's LoC reduction

> What does script accuracy refer to?
I should probably expand on that - just how many of the performance tests were passing on features which are supposed to work. i'll word it differently

> because the way we convert to 4.0 scale at waterloo is kinda cheese
yea technically anything in 80-84% is 3.7/4 so im not entirely sure why it's not continuous and why it jumps to 3.9 post 85% ¯\_(ツ)___/¯ so i'll just write it as 3.8 lmaoo.

i agree with your other points about how many of my points are kinda vague and i should focus more on what's built rather than strategies - and also avoiding my own analysis of my work, i didn't really think of it that way before. thanks again :)

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: im a pretty big fan of this resume overall - is nvidia until apr 2025? timeline doesn't make sense currently - multi-threaded ssh is awkward grammatically. the ssh itself isn't multithreaded, you're just running multiple concurrent ssh sessions (i assume). I would reword to "via several [concurrent or async/asynchronous] SSH sessions" - you have a tendency to list the same technologies many times. your apple block says Java Spring Boot and AWS RDS in every single bullet. It's especially jarring when you say "accelerated ... by using Java Spring Boot, etc, ..." because you bury the lead about what you actually did (migrate stuff to an independent service) behind info i already had. My eyes initially glazed over the back half of the bullet and missed that you explained **how** you got a speedup (which is valuable info) because I saw the tech stack for the 3rd time in a row - "Realized up 2x engineering productivity" - grammar? not sure what this should say. realized up to 2x eng prod gain? Or you improved eng prod up to 2x of original? - IMO putting dates on projects is a bad idea - Sometimes you use the same phrase too close together. Both your algo trading bullets use "leveraging" - You seem to want quant but C++ is your last language listed? - Clang is kind of a nonsense thing to list as like a technology next to your matching engine. This raises a bit of a red flag in my systems engineer brain. - Try to break up some of your 2 line points into 2 single line points. Longer sentences are just harder to read. E.g. your first IBM point is hard for me to parse. I would try "Designed distributed microservice system/architecture for handling shipment data in Java Spring Boot" and then "Achieved a 50% latency reduction when handling high volume data by leveraging Redis caching of shipment groups"

critiques.author: Ming
critiques.content: Thanks for all the valuable feedback! Some questions I have: 1. My nvidia internship is extended so I'm doing my final term in school while doing the internship remotely. So does that raise a red flag in company's eyes? 2. For internship projects that use a lot of the same technology, i.e. my apple internship, how would you recommend I list my technologies? Maybe I just won't put all of them all the time? 3. Why is putting dates on projects a bad idea?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: No problem 1. I don't think that's a big deal. I just pointed out the dates cause you have it as Sep 2024-Apr 2024 right now which is obviously a typo. 2. I think as long as each technology is listed in at least 1 bullet point then you're good. Java Spring Boot and AWS RDS are in the first bullet so they don't need to be in the later ones. Kafka is only in the last bullet so that's fine. 3. The way I see it is that the date doesn't add any value to the project (I don't really care when the project was built, just that you built it) but it does allow me to build a narrative about you. In your case, if I'm a citadel recruiter I might notice that you built 2 very quant-y projects within 2 months during your final year, which signals that you built those projects to get past my resume screen and not for some deep technical curiosity. If your projects were really old, I might read that as a lack of initiative/curiosity since you haven't built anything notable in a while. 9/10 times this won't matter, but it could. Tl;dr, I don't see any upside but I do see possible downside.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- Not enough whitespace. I would cut the entire awards section to add whitespace between the remaining stuff and maybe an extra bullet or two. Not interested in certs or your hs achievements. I could see keeping the 20k schol. I feel like every international resume i see has a scholarship of that size which is why i would cut. You could squeeze it into education to save on the awards heading
- There's something floaty about the way you write bullet points. It's hard to articulate but I find myself reading much more than i'm learning. Some of it is filler language but even the technical language can feel empty. It might be that you're trying to say too much without explaining enough about any one thing.
Like what is multi-node encryption-decryption efficiency? Is everything encrypted as it passes through your pipeline? But you also have to decrypt at every step? Maybe this is some data eng-specific thing. But then how did you actually improve the efficiency of that? Snowflake S3 and Postgres are the basic components of the pipeline, they don't explain the efficiency gain.
Or what was the bounding box and text generation actually for? How does that "enhance document analysis accuracy and efficiency"?
What did your Azure classification model categorize users for? Why do I care that you used "curated datasets"?
What am I supposed to take away from the fact that MongoDB handled "user data, transactions, and biofuel plant information"? A DB stores data, and that's just data. The transition from vague "user data" to "biofuel plant info" is also jarring
- I wouldn't bold niche technologies like Instamojo
- reducing security risks by 40% sounds kinda sus. i feel like you can only say 40% if you know the total number of vulnerabilities in your system. So then why are 60% of them still there? Saying you fixed some specific number of vulnerabilities would be fine.
You also use analyses of your work which are unexplained/unearned. How did your use of Mongo "ensure data integrity and security"? How did your CI/CD pipelines enhance dev efficiency? What made your Chess GUI "cohesive"?

critiques.author: Espresso
critiques.content: Did you end up liking data engineering more that software?

critiques.author: erosion

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
top sections:
- the key skills at the top (time series analysis, etc.) shouldn't be capitalized
- iirc there's a more formal way to state the degree. i normally put it as "__candidate for __ honours bachelor__'s__ of X" - would help to fill out the space a bit more

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
on the vpf point
- can we quantify "a timely manner"? results are good to show!
- maybe remove "the Federation of Students" accountant and just replace with "an external" accountant? Similiarly, mentions of Council and the Society are very mathsoc-coded but maybe wouldn't read well to a recruiter with no mathsoc context
- can we elaborate on what went into training directors of finance? what were the results, what did they need to do? how many directors were you responsible for? answers to these questions can help to make yourself sound more impressive, literally just by having more detail

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
for pmc vpf
- could we split the vpf part into two bullet points? I feel like maintaining the budget and presenting it to be challenged by external stakeholders (council) is significant enough to warrant its own bullet point, just as overseeing cashflows and reimbursements could be

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
for mathsoc director, finance
- we could expand on the bullet points here - how many requests did you audit, what was involved in cash closing procedures.
- might also be worth mentioning that having done this really well was a part leading to you getting the promoted to vpf!

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
for at-large rep
- you could add a part here about reviewing the budget at a highly granular level, and how it gave you experience with challenging departmental representatives (club presidents) on the accuracy of their budgeting

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
for the competitions/conferences:
- the cumc talk is really cool - we could consider adding how many people you presented to? or where it was physically? mention that it's a national conference?

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content: For waterloo works, order your sections as skills, experience, projects, education

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content: Since you already have a professional experience (research assistant), i would keep that in your experience and move Hack the North Below into projects. For your RA position, try and quantify your experiences better. For example, with your first bullet point, you could mention how many people would use your web app, and you should also mention which touls you used to build it. For your third bullet point, reword the beginning as: "Reduced streaming delay of real-time data by 15% by ...". Also when you're bolding, either only bold your metrics or your technology, you're not being consistent with what you're bolding which makes it confusing to follow

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content: I would also try and incorporate some soft skills into your resume as well. For example, if you have papers written as a part of your RA, you could mention that in your resume. For HtN, you could talk about the size of your team, or if you led the development of a certain part of the app

critiques.content: Ig the main issue with the resume is showing the impact of your work, or that impact being lost due to the length of some of your sections

critiques.author: erosion
critiques.content:
ok i think you can tell that there's too much whitespace here lol

is there _anything_ else you could put here? volunteer experience could really help to fill in the space, and demonstrates what you're like as a person

* "after school" needs a hyphen
* the languages after the projects look good, i like the formatting there
* consider using bold our colours to make key terms stand out? see other resumes in this channel for inspiration - often, you'll want to draw attention to key points, metrics, etc
* i think you're burying the lead on the spotify project - creating a recommendation algorithm is no easy feat. could be worth putting that nearer to the top of the section
* same with the assistant instructor section - we spend so much time on "I can interact with technology" but forget the more important part "I can interact with humans" - we've taken the part about teaching students and hidden at the end of the section! maybe let's try moving it up towards the top of the section - if you choose to bold / highlight things, this could be something worth drawing attention to
* again for the mcdonalds section - being able to train and talk to people is big. As a first-year student, they're not expecting you to come in with technical knowledge. What will set you apart is people skills. give the employers a chance to see that you have them!

critiques.author: IceRat
critiques.content: Wow thanks for the quick and detailed response! I have some volunteer experience as a youth ministry leader so I suppose I could add that. I def agree with focusing on people skills rather than technical rn. I’ll keep in mind all ur points, thanks so much!

critiques.content: (external resume)

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
Pretty solid quantity of experience for 1b but communication is kinda sloppy
- Your stats are suspect to me. Reduced formatting time by 75% compared to what? Traditional OCR does not parse equations afaik so how are you comparing your accuracy to it? Improved search latency *and efficiency* by 40%? What is efficiency? I also don't really like the Selenium stat because you probably haven't actually measured the manual search time but not the worst. Looks bad in context
- I feel like there's a lot more to say about LaTeX.ly than the tech stack and dataset size. Parsing images -> latex equations sounds like an architecturally distinct problem from normal OCR since you can't just identify characters in a stream. If there's anything to say about how you made it understand fractions or subexpressions in exponents/subscripts, you should say it
- Looquest is unclear. You're semantic searching over course topics? What's the graph for? What did you actually do to optimize postgres perf?
- The last linkedit bullet after Won Geeshacks is redundant
- Not a fan of descriptive statements like "for seamless equation..." or "streamline sponsor discovery..". It's filler but ok if you need to fill space
I think you've overindexed on cramming stats and keywords over clearly explaining what you built + why it was valuable/interesting. You should even have enough space to do both if you cut from awards, debate club, and fluff

critiques.author: boo
critiques.content:
Tech stack for internship, I don’t see what you used/experience? (Microsoft-partnered solutions is too vague). Same goes for the rest of the bulletpoints

Try to subsitute your non-technical experience with something else, it’s taking up a lot of space if you’re going for SWE roles.

Arduino projects: keep those to embedded roles. Replace with frontend projects

Scalable and responsive webapp is too vague

How did you do observability? Also add github link for both?

Ok for structure:

 techincal experience
technical projects

Non-technical experiences
LA 2 only. It’s pretty crammed

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
I generally don't agree you should keep arduino stuff to embedded roles. The way you're presenting the smartskin project now makes it unclear that your app is interacting with a hw device. You can still focus on the web stuff but you should make it clear that its arduino + web app as an opener.

For your ML projects, how did you actually use the models? There is no mention of how your IDE answers questions or how you interpret data in SparkSense except mentioning ollama and gemini as technologies.

Main comment is that some bullets are too wordy and hard to follow. Try to cut out filler words and split up 1 2-line bullet into 2 1-line bullets where possible. In some cases there's enough filler to cut that a 2-line could become a 1-liner. This is especially bad in your intern experience block

E.g. "Developed a responsive web application using React.js to display information on current risks of eczema flare ups, with over 75% accuracy rate, with real-time notifications using Twillio"
Could be "Developed a React.js app to display risks of eczema flare-ups with over 75% accuracy" next bullet "Sensor data is processed in real-time and warning notifications are sent using Twilio"

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: I would try to add more detail about impact (why/how you built things). A lot of your bullets right now are just "Built X with Y". All I really learn is that you've used Y. I can see that would be hard in some cases, like idk what more there is to say about fixing bugs on a landing page. But there are obvious ones too like "designed .. a search feature" (for what? why? this statement contains almost 0 information). Especially true for your Ludex job. Without discussing impact there's no sense of scale for what you built. Your 16 month job reads like a 4 month internship because of it

critiques.content: I respect questioning the norm but (imo) this format has poor ergonomics and looks too spaced out. highly subjective tho

critiques.content: I think it's true that my jobs kinda read like I didn't do much. It's so hard to write more meaningful stuff because 1. Working in a super early startup (with only seed round) means a lot of my stuff is either poc, or gets discarded a month later when we pivot the business model. 2. My memory bad, I can't even remember where I put my wallet yesterday, and I worked on practically different things every week. 3. I'm the jockey picking up tasks that others don't want either because they use a techstack others don't want to learn, they're legacy code, the tasks is fixing tech debt, or it's a lot of debugging. How do I quantify things like that, maybe in dev hours?

- Indent relevant coursework line

- your right margin is larger than your left.

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content:
Your resume is cracked for first coop lol. But some quick tips:
- I would elaborate on the engineering and technical experience for your extra cirriculars. Rn it just looks like you're grouping radom experiences together and it doesn't make a ton of sense. If all of the bullets are related to the STEM robotics team I would format it as an experience.
- For Waterloo Aerial, what was your role? I would format this as an experience and include your position name, and I would talk a bit about the tools you used while there.
- I feel like you're bolding too much, personally I bold either all of the technologies I used (Ex: Vercel, Groq LLaMA, etc.) or the impact metrics (8-15LLM, 9+ animation states, etc.). Its expecially cluttered when you have the technologies listed on the top.
- In general, I would elaborate on your interpersonal skills a bit - for your first coop many employers are looking for coachability. I would just emphasize this in your bullets involving group work

critiques.author: Kaworu
critiques.content:
Thanks so much for the feedback — and for the kind words about my resume! I really appreciate that.

I just wanted to clarify a couple of things:

For the club and Waterloo Aerial experiences — did you mean it would be better to actually move them into the “Experience” section, or you mean I keep them under “Extracurricular” but format them more like proper experience entries (with a position title and more detailed bullets)? Or use the club name as the title and revise the bullet points so they sound more like a real experience with a defined role.

Also, since I’ve already listed all the technologies under each project, I better remove the bold from all the tech names and only bold the static numbers(like “15+ LLMs”, “9 animation states”, etc.)? Just want to make sure I understood your suggestion correctly.

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content:
- Yeah I would format the experience as more of a role, just because it would hold more weight than just an extracirricular

- Yep, i would just bold the metrics since they show your impact through what work you did. I usually bold the technology but since you've already listed them at the top of each project you dont need to bold them again

critiques.author: Deleted User
critiques.content:
- Remove the react/typescript mention on the top

- IMO for an internship i would move skills to the top, order them as skills, education, experienec, extracirricular, projects.

- I would add UofT Racing and University of Windsor under your experience section rather than extracirricular, since they appear to be more significant/relevant.

- For Watoga, which libraries did you use to process the data? Also the regression model bullet point is missing a metric and doesnt mention which tools you used.

- for optimotive, you listed what you did, but not what impact it had and  what tools you used. Recruitiers want to easily find out which tools you are familiar with and what impact your work had.

- For UofT, same thing - mention what tools you used and try and quantify it in some way (ex: worked in a group of 10...)

- Same with UWindsor, but if you have research published or your name mentioned somewhere, you should link it in your resume!

- I wouldnt list your portfolio website under projects since its fairly minimar compared to your other projects. Move CS Games to your projects section.

- Personally, i add a section to my skills talking about which libraries I know (this might be more relevant to me bc I'm targeting DS)

critiques.author: Boman
critiques.content:
A lot going on in your resume that it's hard to read

- Remove technician and design team experience since they're less relevant. 
- Add metrics to the last bullet point of Hack the North
- Remove technologies beside the titles since they are redundant, the technologies should be reflected from your experience. Plus it hurts your resume severely if you mention technologies that are not in your experience or projects (ex: Kubernetes)
- Don't put ssh as a technology, Linux is fine
- Why is your UNB project separate from UNB experience? If it was part of your job include it in your experience (it's pretty impressive)
- Make sure all your hyperlinks work

critiques.content: across your projects/experiences, highlight relevant keywords or things you want recruiters to notice by bolding them. take a look at other resuymes in this channel to see how this is done

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: This is a weird one cause i have no specific complaints about any of your bullet points, but i also kinda fell asleep reading this. i think your sentences are too formulaic. "Built X with Y technologies [for Z purpose], resulting in W improvement". There's nothing wrong with this structure (i might prefer starting with impact and purpose, and ending in how in many cases) but out of 10 bullet points for your experiences, i would say 9 fit this form. it just gets boring. unconventional resume advice but you could do with some more variety. try to think of a couple new ways to phrase the same things. it could be useful to add some colour about how you built some of these things. what were some big problems you solved? what were some notable features you implemented? there are no glaring questions i have about what you built or how or why, but i think more detail could be interesting and break up the monotony

critiques.author: tristan
critiques.content: tks so much for your suggestion! Is there any bullet point that you think are unclear?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Nah as i said i think every bullet point in isolation is totally fine

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- decreased redundant code by 75% is a bit of a suspicious stat. shouldn't you remove all redundant code? or are you saying you decreased total code size by 75% by dropping the redundant stuff (why was 75% of your code redundant?)
- what does "auditing peer code submissions" mean? who are the peers? what kind of model are you training? is the code the training data? does auditing mean you literally just looked through the code by hand or was there some automated system built here (manual auditing seems lame)?
- try to lead with the impact stats. e.g. cut redundant re-renders by 40% by refactoring.... also, that bullet is off. what are unintentional side effects? how did you measure that 40% of them were gone (i can imagine how you might measure re-renders but not this)? probably just drop the side effct bit altogether. but this line still reads like a string of tech jargon. maybe i'm just not web-pilled enough but idk what makes an event listener blind, or what cache immutability by cloning means, or what this has to do with re-renders. if blind event listeners and cache immutability my cloning is common jargon in webdev world then ignore me

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: you worked on "prediction markets" trading lol? or were you working for a sportsbook (i.e. bet pricing algorithms). just curious.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "bachelors of math, major in cs" may be technically correct if you're in bmath cs but buries the lead. lead with the cs part for most jobs. "[bachelor of/major in] cs, minor in stats"

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "Designed and deployed MLB trading algorithm". this phrase carries a lot of weight to me. you designed the strategy? how good is it? or did you just deploy it? also MLB like you're literally trading prop bets on MLB games right? I hope that would be clear if i knew the company name. otherwise the idea that you wrote an system to do sports betting would be pretty foreign to a lot of people (maybe offputting). i would try my best to divorce the tech stuff that you did from the product you were trading so i don't have to mention it was sports betting.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Your second "in-game algo" bullet is vague. what latency are you measuring? tick to trade? 400ms tick to trade seems pretty bad but depends on the model/time-horizon/product. also, don't just tell me you used concurrency, caching, and a tuned http client (not sure what that one means). try to say a few words about what was concurrent or cached. something like "engineered pipeline that concurrently ingests and evaluates market data" might be one bullet and "cached xyz, achieving 400ms latency" might be another.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "Integrated XGBoost pricing model by compiling lightweight Treelite object and linking via cgo into Go algorithm" ngl but this sounds like 2 hours of work. you compiled a model and linked into it.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: what does bigquery have to do with the algorithm? how did we get to 80% faster predictions?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
sorry this is too late for ww cycle 1. just got around to this
I think the structure got better. i just have some minor gripes about wording:
- ML evaluation to me means evaluating the performance/accuracy of models. I would stick to saying inference
- poll-to-intent throughput should be poll-to-intent latency. you're still measuring the time it takes to perform 1 action, not how many actions you can do in a fixed time
- "standalone pre-event forecasts" is an awkward phrase. I would just drop standalone and say "pre-event forecasts". then you have room to say "by 80% by unifying..." instead of just a comma
- "Powered ML evaluation by designing" seems pointless. "Designed/built/trained XGBoost pricing model and optimized inference using Treelite and cgo" is better to me
- i think first bullet reads better as "Developed an automated trading system for event-driven markets and validated its integrity in live deployment."

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- internal engineer? do you not have a more standard title here?
- your bullet points are too long. longer sentences tend to be harder to parse. my heuristic is you should only have a small handful of 2-liners in your whole resume. easy first step is replace semicolons with line breaks
- From Xms to Yms is weak framing unless the numbers are really bold. prefer multiples/proportions. 150ms to 1ms sounds sick but don't make me do 1300/240 in my head to figure out how significant that was 
- do you have a better name for the accelerator you used for FRC than a TPU? i read that as google TPU but ik it's not that if you had this thing deployed on a FRC bot
- generally, i think there's a lot of fat here. i normally complain that people are too vague ("collaborated with 15 people in an AGILE team"). you include a lot of implementation details, which is an improvement, but is still sometimes irrelevant and obscures more interesting details about what you did. as an exercise, here's my inline comments on your 2 experiences (strikethrough -> redundant imo):
> Implemented a dual-agent architecture: one agent streams fast replies, while a second agent performs memory/RAG
> update and search ~~asynchronously and communicates via RPC~~, reducing response latency from 500ms to 80ms.
> • Partnered with the Letta (MemGPT) engineering team ~~to test a real-time memory SDK~~ **(maybe ok if the SDK is well-known)** for voice agents; identified
> and parallelized a RAG performance bottleneck, cutting latency from 1300ms to 240ms **(what latency is this? how is this different from the last one?)**.
> • Implemented real-time avatar lip-sync and expression rendering by building an audio-to-phoneme mapping pipeline
> ~~and driving mouth/expression parameters from live audio, enabling synchronized real-time virtual chatbot avatars.~~
> • Enabled fully autonomous navigation~~ + scoring~~ for ~~2025 ~~FRC through an AprilTag pose pipeline to transform
> robot-relative pose to global field coordinates~~ via rotational and translational matrices,~~ achieving ±5 inch pose error.
> • Built a vision-guided ~~auto-~~intake system ~~for the 2024 FRC game~~ by deploying a detection model on a TPU processor
> and converting bounding-box detections into robot-relative pose targets for autonomous pickup, winning Innovation in
> Control ~~(Humber College event)~~.
> • Optimized ~~2024 game-piece~~ vision with an OpenCV pipeline (~~color thresholding + ellipse detection~~) while preserving
> detection quality **(measured how? vague)**, cutting latency from 300ms to 120ms and eliminating the need for the TPU processor **(why was this better than TPU?)**.
- other stuff could still be said more concisely if i rearranged the words instead of just striking through. since this is co-op 1, it's fair to keep some fat in there to pad out your resume, but keep in mind for future

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: I do not have any prior work experiences in the field, so my experiences section is a bit empty and unrelated for the average tech-resume standard. I am also interested in applying for math/physics research positions, what kind of things should I be putting on to my resume in that case?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Research as in URA?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: CV matters less; go talk to a prof directly first

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Swap your projects and exp

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Your exp is relevant to game dev, esp the art

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: You’re not writing the art exp well though

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: It sounds like you have a portfolio, link to that in your resume either on that exp, or standalone. Maybe provide direct links to the specific pieces you’ve won awards for, or sold.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: If there are known exhibits you’ve shown your art, list that, and dates (year, minimum)

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: If you were an actual art instructor, you should talk about the courses you taught, and what was in the curriculum/syllabus. also how many students. What was your focus area/specialty in terms of medium and also subject?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Any particular styles you tend to do?

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: You should def drop by the graphics lab Waterloo and chat with the profs there. They have fun problems 😄

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: Any kind of research, work in a physics lab would be cool, and not just the ones from uwaterloo. My grades should be good enough too. URA would be cool

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: I have an Artstation page on the top of my resume, and it has a few of my pencil works and digital drawings, I am pretty proud of them. Also, should I be allocating more space for these art experiences? I don’t view them to be that relevant for SWE and somewhat relevant to game dev jobs.

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: Haha yeah I completely agree with you on this. It is such a pain and so time consuming. (funny for you to say computer is easier tho lol)🫠

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: There are also not too many game dev jobs on UWWorks, so I don’t want to limit myself to only that (ofc I can also apply externally), do you think my resume is strong for a front-end job?

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: Gotcha! I will try and drop in sometimes 😌

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: They’re not relevant for the majority of SWE jobs, BUT you’re clearly very good at it as opposed to mediocre, so you should include it! You never know what opportunities it opens up.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: SWE/computer science at this point is highly cross-functional; knowing a second area of expertise gives you a leg up over the majority of candidates as you’ll come in with that knowledge and it might be relevant and they don’t have to teach you eg principles of art

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Waterloo would be easiest to get that head start in, so go talk to some profs! 🙂 start by looking at their research papers and see if there’s areas of research that interest you.

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: It’s ok but if you have a portfolio set up with examples of frontend pages you’ve designed that would help as well

critiques.author: 𝔹ℤ
critiques.content: I have some things on GitHub, but they are not completely finished, not sure if the employers check them often

critiques.author: shh
critiques.content: Not often, so share the things you don’t mind being looked at 🙂

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: from a very quick glance of your resume, i'm going to be perfectly honest with you, it looks like keyword soup

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: like the "chess game" (which i assume is your CS246 project)

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: remove things like GDB + valgrind + github + ux/ui

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: it actually makes things harder to read and is the first thing i read

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: also get rid of the certifications - they're very pointless, esp if you have projects to show for it

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: throw out slashes for things like C/C++, if it gets passed through a resume parser, it may have no clue wtf to do

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: C and C++ have diverged significantly enough that they're separate languages

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: PL/SQL is fine since thats what its called

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Throw out GitHub from dev tools and mentions of github from your resume (besides links), its literally just one possible remote git server and means absolutely nothing

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Oracle as a Devtool...?????? Oracle is a company

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Throw out Valgrind, thats a given if you do anything with cpp

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Throw out gdb, thats a given if you do anything with cpp

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Throw out UML, thats not a dev tool

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: Your dates and locations are weirdly formatted - it goes over into your margins

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: for your second experience, the first bullet point, even though you mention AI, it's actually extremely weak

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: since a recruiter will read it and just see you as connecting to the openai api and not actually doing anything yourself

critiques.author: NotMyLyfe
critiques.content: first experience, second bullet point has no context

critiques.content: Your first bullet point for Enova is interesting but quite weak in its description so try adding metrics and more descriptive of what you done/impacted

critiques.content: The raspberry pi bullet point.... Says absolutely nothing

critiques.author: Boman
critiques.content: But Overall your Sterimax section is quite good in its description you should take that as an example to improve your other experiences

critiques.content:
- There's too much padding with your header, and i also think you can make your side margins a bit smaller

critiques.content:
- I haven't done independent contracting so idk how its usually formatted on a resume. I would definitely try and quantify your bullet points if possible here to show your impact bc you have a good variety of experiences. I wouldn't list the tools you used beside your project names, instead I would mention them within your bullet points.

- Same with Catholic crossultural services. elaborate on your bullet points more - theres a ton of good potential soft skills here

- pls list your projects section in reverse chronological order

critiques.content:
- 100% of cart data is a weird thing to bold. maybe it was a hard technical problem figuring out how to ingest/use 100% of the data but it means nothing to me. cool project tho. might want to invert it a little and say "built a chrome extension that launches an ace attorney style debate... by interecepting amazon/shopify". my eyes kinda glazed over by the time i got the AI lawyer debate midway thru the 2nd line but that's the fun part.
- i honestly think your projects should go ahead of experience. they help you stand out way better. your experience is pretty normal first/second year cs student stuff and there's not much interesting technicals there. i think you're likely to get written off before people get to the cool part
- try to avoid adjectives like "fast, reliable, streamlined, optimized, secure". they don't tell me anything unless they're substantiated with some kind of data or at least an explanation of how you made it fast/secure/whatever. the phrases "reducing redundancy and optimizing performance", "ensuring secure and reliable operations for server users", etc don't tell me anything. i have to take your word that these are true. these should be self-evident from the rest of the bullet point. give me some reason to believe these features are as good as you say, don't just tell me that they are

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- What is "technical deep dives"? Why is this not just in your skills? Half of those technologies were already mentioned in depth in the resume body too
- Your line spacing is kinda fucked. Some lines are closer together than others.
- Can you make it less cramped? I would completely cut Teranet to give me more breathing room in your spacing and margins
- Some of your bullets get too into the weeds. Try to avoid using language that's too technical or domain specific. I think "Reduced Raft's latency by up to 50%" and "Eliminated up to 3 seconds of db unavailability during fail-overs" communicates all the same stuff in simpler language. Only DB engineers would notice the difference. Also in your version you use present tense for one and past tense for the other. There are a lot of dead words that add a tiny bit of detail at a much larger cost to readability. Another strategy is inverting your bullets so you lead with impact. Your Auvik Kubernetes point should lead with "streamlined..." and then say "by leveraging...". Somebody who's not that familiar with Kubernetes can understand the impact you had and then ignore the details at the end instead of skipping that bullet after hitting the dense technical jargon
Overall this is one of the top resumes i've seen on here. Get a good sense of what you can do, why your work was important, why it was cool. Just needs some clarity
